notes.title: 2026 Social Media Content Creation
notes.content:
# 2026 Social Media Content Creation

- SPCL
   - Status: control or have access to a scarce resource that other people want. Ex) having lots of money means you have a resource being money that everyone wants
   - Power: saying something that when someone complies, leads to something positive. Ex) someone says to buy 10 stocks, and you do and you make lots of moeny
   - Credibility: proof of acomplishment
   - Likeliness/relatability: how relatable you are to someone else and how alike are you. Ex) vibe with each other, personalities match, same gender, same similar values (want to get rich).

- Social media is moving towards interests and what people find most interesting (all social media algorithms to maximize watch time and time spent on app)

- strategy for 2026 is to make 10 pieces of content per day. Either livestream (best) or make a long video (second best), then make shorts from the longer video to draw people to watch the livestream/long video on youtube.
- make interesting videos and will keep watching

notes.title: Caches
notes.content:
# Caches - Appendix B

- Set-associative usually used in GPUs:
  - Set-associative basically good for many threads since many threads=many different sets of reused code at different parts in the program
  - GPUs run thousands of threads at once, and each thread accesses different pieces of data. If the cache were direct-mapped, many threads might try to store their data in the same cache line, causing conflicts and evictions. Set-associative caches let each “set” hold multiple lines, so threads accessing different data can coexist in the cache without constantly kicking each other out. This reduces conflicts and improves performance.
- Instruction cache and data cache are separated for performance since pipeline accesses instruction in fetch stage and data in ex stage at same time, which cache only has one port causing structural hazard
- Cache optimizations - Appendix B2
  - Hierarchy of cache (L1,L2) with increasing size allows for more chance of cache hit since larger cache increases probability (decreasing miss penalty)

notes.title: Fundamentals of Quantitative Design and Analysis
notes.content:
# Chapter 1: Fundamentals of Quantitative Design and Analysis

-   Moores law: #transistors on microchip double every two years. This has been slowing down recently
-   Amdahl's law: performance of multicore processors, increasing # cores eventually leads to diminishing returns![image](/notes/images/image.png)
-   IoT devices are embedded computers that are connected to internet
-   Servers mainly focus on scalability (how much can I scale my server based on increase in demand), high throughput to serve all customers
-   Clusters are collections of servers into one big computer
    -   **Basically we are aiming to maximize throughput efficiently, so as much throughput as possible with the lowest power consumption/cost** (doing the same work for cheaper). Throughput here is defined by:
        -   **Compute capacity** (number of cores/ALUs/FPUs)
        -   **Memory bandwidth** (can data feed the compute units fast enough, **Feed pipe**?)
        -   **Interconnect bandwidth** (GPU-to-GPU or node-to-node communication-**nvlink**)
        -   **Parallelism exploited** (ILP, thread-level, data-level, model parallelism)
    -   Important to be best in price-performance (lowest inference cost/ price per token-nvidia vera rubin focuses on this since the cost reduction saves millions).
    -   Memory bandwidth (highly parallel systems are bounded by memory bandwidth - feed system with large enough pipe)
-   Parallelism at different levels of abstraction
    -   Data level parallelism (SIMD has hundreds of workers/ALUs doing the same task on multiple data)-same action done on multiple pieces of data. 
    -   Instruction level parallelism (superscalar/out of order exec/pipelining (exec multiple independent instructions at same time)): instructions are independent of each other and can be done in parallel
    -   Thread Level Parallelism (Simultaneous multithreading SMT): running multiple threads on different cores in parallel
-   **Computer Architecture** refers to ISA, microarch, and hardware.
    -   Microarch is the architecture and organization of the computer (design of the system)
    -   Hardware is physical implementation of microarch. Better hardware with same microarch can lead to better performance (**Better hardware** → faster transistors, higher clock speeds, bigger/faster caches, better memory system, more cores, or more reliable packaging.)
-   Architecting with new technology/scalability in mind is important
    -   Also taking into account the fact that bandwidth is scaling by x^2 compared to latency (ML and graphics applications are integral to this trend since bandwidth throttles throughput and throughput is extremely important for AI workloads due to the extremely high quantity of data processing needed)
-   Bandwidth is theoretical, throughput is actual
-   We can no longer improve performance mainly by increasing clock frequency or simply packing more transistors onto a chip, because power and heat limits prevent us from using them all simultaneously. So modern computer architecture improves performance through architectural techniques instead of raw scaling such as **parallelization, ASIC, energy efficient design**
-   Modern AI hardware design is about **maximizing throughput per dollar and per watt while keeping chip size and datacenter power costs manageable**
-   Modern AI/ML and graphics performance is primarily evaluated by **throughput, latency, energy efficiency, cost efficiency, and memory bandwidth**, with **throughput and performance per watt** usually being the most critical because i want my models running as fast as possible (highest throughput) and as cheaply as possible (highest margin).
-   Focus on common case when designing. Make common case fast, and infrequent case

notes.title: Growing instagram
notes.content:
# Growing instagram

-   Have a niche
-   Find trending content in the niche, make 10 videos with a few different variations of each, test them, find a pattern between the top 3 performing ones, keep posting that pattern
-   Hook should be tailored towards grabbing the interest of your niche. I watched a video ad because I was interested in them since I am competing against them

[https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=JLfkBRRvtPc](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=JLfkBRRvtPc)

-   Like and save content in my niche to train account to show me only that niche
-   Find trending audio first, edit around it
-   Add polls and engage with audience![image](/notes/images/image-1.png)
-   Caption: ![](https://mail.google.com/mail/u/0?ui=2&ik=71d65d317f&attid=0.2&permmsgid=msg-f:1859508410103707478&th=19ce4d0c01153756&view=fimg&fur=ip&permmsgid=msg-f:1859508410103707478&sz=s0-l75-ft&attbid=ANGjdJ_z15mARHQTSCgfw2Pvj17xDtBI9oG-HaDbCuGZoMhd8lV1azffnErFRhNghWpqHI3wqeRlxITEFx4RFwU5la84fVhfEHUDzOJ0NNHdD2tavs211bXpbqgXAvg&disp=emb&zw)
-   Pin CTA on top performing reels
    -   Use bio to explain why they should follow

-   Use keywords that relate to niche, describe account (what I do), value I provide and why should follow, and community (join the community)

-   Put keywords on videos
-   Core audience needs to find interesting, valuable, entertaining

-   Insta algo:

    -   Pushes out incrementally to more people based on completion time/watch time/retention/replays, shares, saves, comments, likes, follows after watching
    -   Compares performance to other reels that were tested in that niche, if good then pushes further
    -   Audience match targeting based on captions and video transcription

notes.title: Instruction Set Architecture
notes.content:
# ISA - Appendix A {1-8}

  - ISA architecture directly affects computer arch
    - RISC vs CISC: RISC uses simpler, uniform instructions → simpler pipelines, easier decoding, higher throughput.
    - Instruction encoding:
      - Fixed-length (e.g., 32-bit) → simpler fetch/decode, better pipeline performance.
      - Variable-length (e.g., 8/16/32-bit) → smaller code size but more complex decoding hardware.
      - Code size vs performance tradeoff: variable encoding improves memory efficiency; fixed encoding improves execution speed.
    - Compilers strongly affect performance because they determine instruction scheduling, register allocation, and how efficiently the ISA is used.
    - Supported data types/specialized instructions: vector/SIMD for ML applications

notes.title: Markdown Guide
notes.content:
# Markdown Guide

A quick reference for Markdown syntax supported in Notes.

## Text Formatting

**Bold**, *italic*, ~~strikethrough~~, `inline code`

## Headings

Use `#` through `######` for headings.

## Lists

- Unordered item
- Another item
  - Nested item

1. Ordered item
2. Second item

## Links

- External: [OpenAI](https://openai.com)
- Wiki link to another note: [[Welcome to Notes]]

## Code Blocks

```js
const greet = (name) => `Hello, ${name}!`
console.log(greet('world'))
```

## Blockquotes

> This is a blockquote.

## Tables

| Column A | Column B |
|----------|----------|
| Cell 1   | Cell 2   |
| Cell 3   | Cell 4   |

## Horizontal Rule

---

Back to [[Welcome to Notes]].

notes.title: Modern CPU Concepts, Definitions, and more
notes.content:
# Modern CPU Concepts, Definitions, and more

## [NVIDIA Vera CPU](https://www.nvidia.com/en-us/data-center/vera-cpu/)

## Concepts and Definitions

### AI
- Edge AI: running AI algorithms, such as neural networks, on local hardware (edge of a network) rather than running through the cloud. For example, running computer vision models right on a smartphone via accelerated hardware.


### Hardware
- KV cache: key-value cache is a crucial technique for accelerating Large Language Model (LLM) inference by storing the keys and values of previous tokens in memory
- [HPC High performance compute](https://www.nvidia.com/en-us/glossary/high-performance-computing/)
- Spatial Multithreading: multithreading by statically allocating hardware for each thread. This still makes no sense since to me, dynamic allocation would be better since we would be using as many functional units whereas static allocation we might have functional units that are unused if that thread doesnt contain such operations.

notes.title: Pipelineing
notes.content:
# Pipelining - Appendix C


- Pipelining takes advantage of parallelism between the actions needed to execute instructions (washing machine/assembly line analogy)
  - Pipelining increases the processor instruction throughput—the number of instructions completed per unit of time—but it does not reduce the execution time of an individual instruction. In fact, it usually slightly increases the execution time of each instruction due to overhead in the control of the pipeline. The increase in instruction throughput means that a program runs faster and has lower total execution time, even though no single instruction runs faster!

notes.title: Sales and business 101
Verilator
notes.content:
# q1 verilator install

- Verilator basically compiles verilog code into c++ so that it can run much faster than other simulators
- When top->eval() is called, this basically halts main, and runs the verilog c++ thread with all updated variables. Its important to note that verilator optimizes c++ code so sometimes updates wil l still occur in hardware even if eval is not called since behind the scenes, things like assign/wires are just references so they update automatically even without eval being called.

notes.title: Welcome to Notes
notes.content:
# Welcome to Notes

This is your personal knowledge base. You can write notes in **Markdown** and link them together.

## Features

- **Folders** — organize notes into nested folders
- **Topics** — tag notes for cross-folder filtering
- **[[Wiki Links]]** — link notes together with `[[Note Title]]`
- **Backlinks** — see which notes reference the current one
- **Search** — quickly find any note

## Getting Started

Create a new note with the **+** button next to any folder. Add tags to cross-reference across folders.

Try linking to [[Markdown Guide]] to see how wiki-style links work.
